sources.notes: Read statement and constraints: same hidden bracket oracle as C1/C2, but query limit is 100.
Used official editorial hard-version code and discussion: choose a sequence of block weights based on triangular counts so one query decodes many positions.
Used as an additional cross-check for the C3 idea: query design distinguishes multiple brackets by unique contribution sets.

reference_candidates.notes: Candidate Luogu solution page; not consulted in this review.

annotation.summary: Interactive reconstruction of a hidden bracket sequence under the hardest 100-query limit.
annotation.constraints: n <= 1000, at most 100 queries, k <= 1000, repeated indices allowed.
annotation.core_idea: After finding opposite anchors, one query must decode a larger block than in C2. The hard solution chooses lengths/weights whose triangular-number contributions are strictly decodable: when an unknown is '(', its local pattern contributes a known triangular value to f(t); when it is ')', it contributes zero. Choose a set of such weights with increasing cumulative sums so the total answer can be greedily decoded back into all brackets of the block.
annotation.complexity: O(log n + n / b) queries for a larger constant block size b; within 100 queries; O(n) local decoding.
tricks: Use triangular-number contribution weights created by repeated anchor patterns.
Greedily decode a unique subset sum from the oracle answer.
The query-length limit k <= 1000 constrains the choice of weights.

solution_variants.name: triangular-weight-decoding
solution_variants.summary: Choose a list of pattern lengths p_j so that each '(' contributes p_j(p_j+1)/2 and the sum is uniquely decodable; decode a large block per query.
solution_variants.complexity: O(log n + n / b) queries, within 100

solution_variants.name: medium-binary-coding-fallback
solution_variants.summary: The C2 binary-coded block method works conceptually but does not pack enough positions for the hard query limit.
solution_variants.complexity: O(log n + n / b) queries with smaller b; not sufficient for 100 in worst case

tags.evidence: The problem is solved by designing and decoding a limited number of interactor queries.
The f(t) oracle is forced to behave like a weighted sum over unknown bracket states by a carefully constructed query string.
tags.description: Designing queries to an oracle/interactor so that the numeric or Boolean response directly encodes the desired hidden information.
tags.created_reason: The broad interactive tag does not distinguish problems whose main difficulty is constructing a decoding oracle rather than binary search or randomized probing.

tags.evidence: The hard version's main difficulty is packing many bracket bits into one returned integer via uniquely decodable triangular weights.
tags.description: Encoding multiple hidden decisions into one numeric/query response using unique weights, positional codes, or other decodable representations.
tags.created_reason: Interactive and reconstruction tasks often hinge on how information is packed into few oracle answers; this is more specific than generic bitmask or constructive tags.

tags.evidence: The decodable weights are triangular numbers counting regular substrings contributed by repeated patterns.

The official tag includes dp; one way to view/check the chosen weights is as a unique subset-sum/code design over possible contributions, though the submitted solution can greedily decode due to the chosen sequence.
Binary search is used for the initial anchor calibration before heavy block decoding.
The answer of each query represents multiple hidden binary states, generalizing the bitmask decoding of the medium version.
The algorithm constructs a concrete family of query strings satisfying query-length and uniqueness constraints.
